🎓 IB MYP4 Individuals & Societies

History & Future of Money

From Barter to Bitcoin: 11,000 Years of Monetary Evolution

📖 12 Topics
🏛️ Global Context: Orientation in Space & Time
💡 Key Concept: Systems

🎯 IB MYP Framework

Statement of Inquiry

"Change is shaped by the relationship between causality and innovation, as shifts in space and time influence the evolution of ideas and societies."

Global Context

Orientation in Space and Time: Examining the 11,000-year trajectory of exchange from barter to digital currency.

Key Concept

Systems: Understanding the interconnected structures of central banking and blockchain networks.

Related Concepts

Innovation and Causality: How technological breakthroughs drive monetary evolution.

Assessment Focus

Criterion D: Thinking Critically - Evaluating the legitimacy of cryptocurrency as a medium of exchange.

📜 Evolution of Money: A Timeline

🌍

~9,000 BCE

Barter Systems

Early agricultural societies engaged in direct exchange of goods. The "double coincidence of wants" problem limited trade.

~1,200 BCE

Commodity Money

Shells, seeds, cocoa beans, and livestock became widely accepted mediums of exchange. Durable, portable, and divisible.

~600 BCE

Lydian Coinage

King Croesus of Lydia introduces the first standardized coins made of electrum. The royal stamp guarantees value.

~500 BCE - 400 CE

Classical Coinage

Athenian "Owl" coins and Roman denarius. Coinage becomes political messaging. Roman debasement leads to inflation.

600 CE

Paper Money in China

Chinese merchants develop paper money as "bills of credit." Later adopted in Europe.

1690 CE

Colonial Paper Money

Massachusetts Bay Colony issues first Western paper money to pay soldiers. Represents "representative value."

1971 CE

End of Bretton Woods

Nixon ends gold convertibility. The fiat currency era begins - money has no intrinsic value, only trust.

2009 CE

Bitcoin

Satoshi Nakamoto launches Bitcoin - the first decentralized cryptocurrency. Blockchain technology emerges.

🤝 Barter & Early Exchange

The Double Coincidence of Wants

The fundamental problem with barter: both parties must want exactly what the other has at the same time and place. This severely limited trade opportunities.

Commodity Money Solution

Societies developed items that were:

  • ✓ Durable: Can be stored and used over time
  • ✓ Portable: Easy to transport
  • ✓ Divisible: Can be split into smaller units
  • ✓ Widespread: Recognized and valued by many

The Yap Stone: A Proto-Ledger

The massive limestone disks of Yap (Micronesia) demonstrate that money's value is a social contract, not physical reality. Even when stones were lost at sea, ownership remained valid through oral history!

Double Coincidence of Wants

Both parties must have what the other wants simultaneously.

Commodity Money

Physical objects with intrinsic value used as currency.

Unit of Account

A standard measurement for comparing values of different goods.

🪙 The Rise of Coinage

The Lydian Innovation (600 BCE)

King Croesus introduced coins made of electrum (gold-silver alloy) with a royal stamp. This revolutionary change:

  • ✓ Reduced transaction costs
  • ✓ Eliminated need to weigh/assay metal
  • ✓ Internalized trust in the state
  • ✓ Created standardized unit of account

Coinage as Political Messaging

Athenian Owl

Displaying wisdom and economic dominance of Athens. The most recognized coin of the classical world.

Roman Denarius

Featured Emperor's likeness to establish presence in distant provinces. First used for political propaganda.

Monetary Debasement

As Roman expenses grew, emperors reduced silver content in coins. This led to:

↑ Expenses → ↓ Silver Content → ↑ Prices → ↓ Public Confidence

This mirrors modern concerns about fiat currency expansion and inflation!

📄 From Paper to Fiat

The Paper Revolution

Paper money originated in China (600 CE) but saw Western development in Massachusetts Bay Colony (1690), issued as "bills of credit" to pay soldiers.

Fiat Money: The Modern Era

Intrinsic Value → Representative Value → Fiat Value

In 1971, Nixon ended the gold convertibility of the dollar, ending the Bretton Woods system. Modern currencies are "free-floating" - their value depends on:

  • • Central bank policy credibility
  • • Economic health of the issuing nation
  • • Market supply and demand

Fiat Money

Currency with no intrinsic value, not backed by gold or commodities.

Bretton Woods System

Post-WWII monetary system linking currencies to the US dollar, which was convertible to gold.

Free-Floating

Currency value determined by market forces, not government fixing.

🔍 OPVL: Source Analysis Framework

The gold standard for source evaluation in IB History. Use this to analyze any source on the history of money.

Element Key Questions Application to "History of Money" Video
Origin (O) Who created it? When? What institutional context? Caroline Turco, Curator at ANA Money Museum. Non-profit educational organization chartered by US Congress.
Purpose (P) Why was it created? Who is the target audience? Educational overview for general public; institutional promotion to encourage museum visits.
Value (V) What unique insights does it offer? Usefulness? Visual evidence of physical artifacts (Yap stones, Lydian coins). Shows continuity of money functions across cultures.
Limitation (L) What is missing? What biases exist? Artifact-centric focus; less on modern banking or digital currency. Western-centric narrative may overlook Global South.

₿ Cryptocurrency & Blockchain

What is Cryptocurrency?

A digital currency operating on decentralized networks using cryptography for security. No central authority like banks or governments.

How Blockchain Works

Distributed Ledger

Transactions recorded across thousands of computers (nodes) worldwide.

Immutable

Once recorded, data cannot be altered without compromising the entire chain.

Consensus

Network verifies transactions through Proof-of-Work or Proof-of-Stake.

Decentralized

No single authority controls the network - trust is in code, not institutions.

Proof-of-Work vs. Proof-of-Stake

Aspect Proof-of-Work (PoW) Proof-of-Stake (PoS)
Mechanism Miners solve complex puzzles Validators lock up coins as collateral
Energy Use Very High (like mid-sized nations) 99% lower than PoW
Examples Bitcoin, Dogecoin Ethereum, Cardano
Speed Slower (~7 TPS) Faster (thousands TPS)

⚖️ Functions of Money: Can Crypto Compete?

Function Fiat Currency Cryptocurrency (Bitcoin)
Medium of Exchange High: Widely accepted, fast retail transactions Low-Moderate: High fees during peaks, limited acceptance
Unit of Account High: Stable prices, predictable value Low: Extreme volatility makes pricing difficult
Store of Value Moderate: Subject to inflation over time High Potential/Low Stability: "Digital gold" but volatile

⚔️ The Debate: Is Cryptocurrency Legitimate?

✓ Arguments FOR Cryptocurrency

  • Financial Inclusion: Banks the "unbanked" - 4 billion people without bank access
  • Efficiency: Instant 24/7/365 settlement, lower fees than traditional banking
  • Transparency: Public ledger prevents corruption
  • Programmable Money: Smart contracts automate transactions (DeFi)
  • Borderless: Send money anywhere without intermediaries
  • Privacy: Pseudonymous transactions protect identity

✗ Arguments AGAINST Cryptocurrency

  • Deflationary Risk: Fixed supply (MV=PY) causes recession
  • Environmental Damage: PoW mining = energy consumption of nations
  • Illegal Activities: Money laundering, terrorism financing
  • Volatility: Not a reliable store of value
  • No Consumer Protection: Lost keys = lost funds forever
  • Scalability: Bitcoin TPS ~7 vs. Visa thousands

📊 PESTEL Analysis: Evaluating Cryptocurrency

A framework for macro-environment analysis in your IB essays.

P

Political

El Salvador: Bitcoin legal tender. China: Ban. Regulation varies globally.

E

Economic

Volatility vs. utility. Correlation with stocks questions "safe haven" status.

S

Social

Millennials/Gen Z trust digital assets. "Dark tetrad" correlation research.

T

Technological

Lightning Network for scalability. Security of code vs. exchange hacks.

E

Environmental

PoW energy crisis. Ethereum's PoS transition shows solution exists.

L

Legal

EU MiCA regulation. US SEC vs. Ripple. Patchwork global laws.

🏦 CBDCs: The State Response

Central Bank Digital Currencies

Governments are developing their own digital currencies - combining crypto efficiency with state stability.

Advantages

State-backed stability, instant settlement, programmable money, easier monetary policy.

Concerns

Surveillance: Every transaction trackable. Programmable spending limits. Privacy loss.

The Surveillance Paradox

Crypto was invented for privacy and freedom from state control. But CBDCs give governments MORE surveillance power than cash ever did!

📝 Test Your Knowledge

Quick Quiz: History of Money

1. What is the "double coincidence of wants" problem in barter?

A. Both parties must have the same amount of money
B. Both parties must want exactly what the other has at the same time
C. Both parties must agree on a fair price
D. Both parties must live in the same location

2. What was the key innovation of Lydian coinage?

A. Using gold instead of silver
B. The royal stamp guaranteed value without weighing
C. Making coins round instead of square
D. Using paper instead of metal

3. What year did Nixon end the gold convertibility of the dollar?

A. 1944
B. 1965
C. 1971
D. 1989

4. In OPVL, what does the "L" stand for?

A. Location
B. Language
C. Limitation
D. Logic

5. What is a key environmental concern with Bitcoin?

A. It uses too little energy
B. Proof-of-Work mining consumes massive electricity
C. It encourages paper money use
D. Coins are made from non-recyclable materials

✓ Answers: 1-B, 2-B, 3-C, 4-C, 5-B

📋 Key Takeaways

IB MYP Assessment Criteria

Criterion Focus In This Unit
A Knowing & Understanding Economic theories, terminology, graph interpretation
B Investigating Case studies, data analysis on money evolution
C Communicating Clear explanations, proper terminology
D Thinking Critically Analyzing cryptocurrency debate, policy evaluation

Essential Vocabulary

Scarcity

Limited resources vs. unlimited wants

Fiat Money

Currency with no intrinsic value

Blockchain

Distributed, immutable ledger

OPVL

Origin, Purpose, Value, Limitation

PESTEL

Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal

CBDC

Central Bank Digital Currency

Start Assessment →
